title: Export Controls Become Bargaining Chips as AI Distillation War Escalates
subhead: The US sat on 100+ approved Entity List entries for eight months, Anthropic accuses Alibaba of the largest known AI model extraction, and the G7 tries to build mineral alliances faster than China can weaponize supply chains.

sections.heading: US Approved DeepSeek Blacklisting — Then Did Nothing for Eight Months
sections.body: An interagency committee cleared DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese firms for the Entity List last year. None have been listed. Reuters reported that the US has added no new entities since October 2025 — the longest gap since the blacklist became a serious enforcement tool. Commerce Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler has avoided new listings for fear of escalation, according to the report. The reason: China controls the rare earth supply that US defense and chip firms depend on, and the blacklist has become a bargaining chip held in reserve rather than a security instrument applied on schedule. Some of the unlisted firms allegedly supplied components for Russian drones recovered in Poland, while others sold restricted Nvidia chips into Chinese universities. Anthropic and OpenAI both told US officials that DeepSeek tried to extract capabilities from their models. The case for listing is strong; the case for waiting is purely diplomatic.
sections.whyItMatters: Export controls now flex with the trade cycle, not the threat cycle. Beijing can read the pause as confirmation that pressure on rare earths buys breathing room on chips.

sections.heading: Anthropic Accuses Alibaba of Largest Known AI Distillation Attack
sections.body: In a June 10 letter to Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott and Ranking Member Elizabeth Warren, Anthropic alleged that operators linked to Alibaba and its Qwen AI lab ran the largest known distillation campaign against a frontier AI model. Using nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts, the operators generated more than 28.8 million exchanges with Claude between April 22 and June 5, targeting agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon planning capabilities. Anthropic framed the campaign as both an IP theft issue and a national security concern, arguing that distilled capabilities could accelerate China's military and cyber AI. The letter follows a February disclosure in which Anthropic named DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax as having generated more than 16 million Claude exchanges through roughly 24,000 fraudulent accounts. Anthropic urged Congress to expand intelligence-sharing between frontier AI developers and the government, clarify antitrust rules to allow distillation-attack coordination, and impose penalties on companies responsible for large-scale model extraction. Alibaba's Hong Kong-listed shares dropped sharply after the reports.
sections.whyItMatters: Distillation doesn't require stealing model weights or breaking into servers — just flooding the model with queries. The legal and technical boundaries of this form of extraction remain unsettled, and the US government is still figuring out whether to treat it as trade theft, espionage, or something in between.

sections.heading: US Partially Lifts Anthropic Export Ban — Mythos 5 Restored for Select Users, Fable 5 Remains Blocked
sections.body: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick confirmed in a June 26 letter that the US government has partially lifted its export ban on Anthropic's frontier models. Claude Mythos 5, the cybersecurity-focused model, will again be accessible to roughly 100 US critical infrastructure organizations. Fable 5 remains blocked. The partial rollback comes two weeks after the original ban, imposed in early June over national security concerns that the models' vulnerability-finding capabilities could be weaponized. France's President Macron said at the G7 that he expects progress on broadening Mythos access, and G7 leaders discussed granting 'trusted partners' access to advanced US AI models like Anthropic's, according to three diplomatic sources cited by Reuters. Austria has asked the EU to consider hosting Anthropic to preserve European access. Meanwhile, OpenAI confirmed it had restricted its own cybersecurity AI model at the government's request, saying it is 'starting with a limited preview for a small group of trusted partners.' Chinese researchers claim Zhipu AI's GLM-5.2 open-weight model can match Mythos in certain bug-finding tasks, according to The Verge.
sections.whyItMatters: The partial rollback shows the tension between locking down powerful AI and keeping it available for the defenders who need it. The fact that Chinese open-weight models are claiming parity on cybersecurity tasks makes the export restriction debate more acute — limiting US access may not limit adversary capability.

sections.heading: China Blacklists 20 More Japanese Entities as Rare Earth Squeeze on Japan Deepens
sections.body: China's Ministry of Commerce added 20 Japanese entities to its export-control list and placed another 20 on a watch list, citing Japan's 'remilitarisation' and attempts to 'acquire nuclear weapons.' The newly listed entities include Japan's National Institute for Defense Studies and military research institutes for ground, naval, and air weapons systems, SCMP reported. The move doubles the number of Japanese entities subject to curbs. It comes alongside China's throttling of key mineral exports to Japan — rare earth shipments have been restricted since PM Sanae Takaichi angered Beijing last November with comments about Taiwan. Canada is seizing the opening: it has given a C$7 million grant to Greenland's Malmbjerg molybdenum mining project, a defense-critical metal, making it the first G7 country to fund a Greenland mine after Trump's takeover push. Canada also announced up to C$73 million in investments for 12 critical mineral projects on June 29. At the G7, Takaichi proposed a Joint Stockpiling Cooperation framework for critical minerals among G7 and like-minded nations.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is weaponizing mineral supply chains against Japan in real time. The question is whether alternative sources and stockpiling agreements can be scaled fast enough to matter — Canada's Greenland bet is a C$7 million signal in a multi-billion-dollar problem.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens 100% Tariff on Any Country Imposing Digital Services Tax
sections.body: President Trump threatened a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies, escalating transatlantic trade tension just days after EU countries met his July 4 deadline to cut tariffs on US goods. The threat would supersede any previously negotiated trade deals, Trump wrote on social media. France's President Macron responded that France would not bow to pressure and scrap its digital tax. Britain has levied a 2% digital services tax since 2020. The EU's Olof Gill called unilateral measures 'unjustified' and said the EU would 'respond swiftly and decisively.' The EU finalized a trade deal in May capping most tariffs at 15%, but digital taxes were excluded from that agreement and remain the sticking point. Trump also threatened 100% tariffs on French wine and champagne in a New York Post interview unless France drops the tax.
sections.whyItMatters: The digital tax dispute sits at the intersection of trade policy and tech sovereignty. European governments see DST as correcting a misalignment between where profits are taxed and where value is created; the US sees it as discrimination against American tech giants. Neither side is backing down.

sections.heading: Team Gen 6 Launches After FCAS Collapse — Europe's Fighter Future Fragments
sections.body: An Airbus-led consortium of eight German defense firms — including Hensoldt, Diehl Defense, MBDA Germany, MTU Aero Engines, and Rohde & Schwarz — launched Team Gen 6 at the ILA Berlin airshow, aiming to develop a sixth-generation fighter jet under German and Spanish leadership. The initiative follows the effective collapse of the Franco-German Future Combat Air System (FCAS), a program estimated at $116 billion, amid acrimony between Paris and Berlin. Spanish firms including Indra, ITP Aero, and Sener are integrated into the effort. Airbus Defense chief Jean-Brice Dumont acknowledged 'the world in 2026 is very different to the world of 2017 when the programme was launched,' saying the reshaped effort needs 'faster milestones.' The project still needs political backing, funding commitments, and likely more partners. Meanwhile, a new GAO report found that only 25% of the US F-35 fleet is fully mission capable, down from 38% in fiscal 2021. The F-35 program office estimates it needs $13.7 billion more through 2031 to reach 80% mission capable rates.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's next-gen fighter ambitions have fragmented at exactly the moment when the US flagship stealth fighter fleet is at its least reliable. Team Gen 6 is a statement of industrial intent, but without government funding and additional partners, it remains a positioning paper.

sections.heading: Iran's Cyberattacks on Israel Tripled — and There's No Ceasefire in Cyberspace
sections.body: Israel logged roughly 4,800 hostile cyber incidents in June 2026, up from about 1,600 in June 2025, according to Yossi Karadi, director general of Israel's National Cyber Directorate. Speaking to the German newspaper Die Welt, Karadi said attacks targeted critical infrastructure, large organizations, SMEs, and ordinary citizens — including law firms and accounting firms that hold sensitive data without enterprise-level security budgets. He said Israel has so far fended off attacks on its most critical systems but acknowledged that 'some groups are very skilled' and that Iranian-linked hackers are coordinating more closely than before. Karadi's framing was blunt: 'Unlike in the kinetic realm, there's no ceasefire in cyberspace.' He did not publish a breakdown of how the 4,800 figure divides between serious intrusions and low-grade probing. In parallel, Australia doubled the maximum penalty for social media platforms that fail to keep under-16s off their services to A$99 million, and the UK announced a full social media ban for under-16s starting in spring 2027, with Prime Minister Starmer calling it going 'further than any country in the world.'
sections.whyItMatters: Iran has shown it can sustain and escalate a cyber campaign even while a kinetic ceasefire holds. The 3x year-on-year increase and improved coordination suggest a permanent, expanding digital front that operates regardless of diplomatic breakthroughs.

bottomLine: The thread connecting this week's developments is the weaponization of interdependence. The US is holding Entity List designations hostage to trade negotiations while China throttles rare earth exports to Japan and distills American AI models at industrial scale. Anthropic's disclosure that 28.8 million fraudulent Claude exchanges came from Alibaba operators shows distillation is no longer a theoretical concern — it's a sustained, well-resourced campaign. Meanwhile, the G7's new critical minerals alliance and Canada's Greenland gambit are moves toward supply chain diversification, but building processing capacity takes years that Beijing's export controls don't grant. The digital tax standoff between the US and Europe, the fragmentation of Europe's fighter jet programs, and Iran's tripling of cyberattacks on Israel all point in the same direction: the architecture of interdependence that defined the post-Cold War order is being actively stress-tested, and the results are not reassuring.
sources: Reuters, Yahoo News, Forbes, SCMP, AP News, TNW, Politico, GAO, Defense News, The War Zone, CryptoBriefing, Straits Times, Japan Times, MSN, The Verge, Decrypt, Defense One, BBC, CNN, The Guardian, TechRepublic, The Deep Dive
